section.title: The Two Witnesses and the Seventh Trumpet
section.sectionRationale: This chapter completes the interlude begun in chapter 10 and closes out the whole trumpet cycle. John measures the temple and its worshipers while the outer courtyard is given to trampling nations, then two empowered witnesses prophesy in sackcloth, are killed by the beast from the Abyss, and are publicly vindicated. The chapter ends with the seventh trumpet, at which loud voices in heaven declare that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and His Christ, the true climax the sixth trumpet's woes were building toward.
section.thesis: God measures and protects His true worshipers even while the nations trample the outer court, and His two witnesses, empowered to prophesy at great cost, are killed and gloated over but then publicly raised and taken up, a preview of the moment the seventh trumpet announces: the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.
section.frameNote: Five beats: measuring the temple and surrendering the courtyard to the nations; the two witnesses empowered to prophesy for 1,260 days; their death and public disgrace at the hands of the beast from the Abyss; their resurrection, ascension, and the earthquake that follows; and the seventh trumpet, announcing that the kingdom has come and the time for judgment and reward has arrived.

units.label: Measuring the temple
units.note: the worshipers counted, the courtyard given over

verses.text: Then I was given a measuring rod like a staff and was told, "Go and measure the {{naos|temple}} of God and the altar, and count the number of worshipers there.
But exclude the courtyard outside the temple. Do not measure it, because it has been given over to the nations, and they will trample the holy city for 42 months.

units.label: The two witnesses empowered to prophesy
units.note: olive trees and lampstands before the Lord

verses.text: And I will empower my two {{martys|witnesses}}, and they will prophesy for 1,260 days, clothed in sackcloth."
These witnesses are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.
If anyone wants to harm them, fire proceeds from their mouths and devours their enemies. In this way, anyone who wants to harm them must be killed.

These witnesses have power to shut the sky so that no rain will fall during the days of their prophecy, and power to turn the waters into blood and to strike the earth with every kind of plague as often as they wish.

units.label: Their death and public disgrace
units.note: the beast from the Abyss makes war on them

verses.text: When the two witnesses have finished their testimony, the beast that comes up from the {{abyssos|Abyss}} will wage war with them, and will overpower and kill them.
Their bodies will lie in the street of the great city, figuratively called Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was also crucified.
For three and a half days all peoples and tribes and tongues and nations will view their bodies and will not permit them to be laid in a tomb.
And those who dwell on the earth will gloat over them, and will celebrate and send one another gifts, because these two prophets had tormented them.
But after the three and a half days, the breath of life from God entered the two witnesses, and they stood on their feet, and great fear fell upon those who saw them.
And the witnesses heard a loud voice from heaven saying, "Come up here." And they went up to heaven in a cloud as their enemies watched them.
And in that hour there was a great earthquake, and a tenth of the city collapsed. Seven thousand were killed in the quake, and the rest were terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven.
The second woe has passed. Behold, the third woe is coming shortly.

units.label: The seventh trumpet: the kingdom has come

verses.text: Then the seventh angel sounded his trumpet, and loud voices called out in heaven: "The {{basileia|kingdom}} of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, and He will reign forever and ever."
And the twenty-four elders who sit on their thrones before God fell on their faces and {{proskyneo|worshiped}} God,
saying: "We give thanks to You, O Lord God Almighty, the One who is and who was, because You have taken Your great power and have begun to reign.

The nations were enraged, and Your wrath has come. The time has come to judge the dead and to reward Your servants the prophets, as well as the saints and those who fear Your name, both small and great, and to destroy those who destroy the earth."

Then the temple of God in heaven was opened, and the ark of His covenant appeared in His temple. And there were flashes of lightning, and rumblings, and peals of thunder, and an earthquake, and a great hailstorm.

naos.gloss: The inner sanctuary or temple proper, the holy dwelling place of God, as distinct from the surrounding outer courts. John is told to measure this sanctuary and its worshipers, marking off God's true, protected people even while the outer courtyard is left unmeasured and handed over to trampling by the nations.

martys.gloss: A witness, one who testifies to what is true, often at the cost of suffering; the word gives us the English martyr, since faithful testimony repeatedly led to death. The two witnesses embody this pattern exactly, empowered to speak for God and killed for it, then publicly vindicated.

abyssos.gloss: The Abyss, the bottomless pit, a place of imprisonment for hostile spiritual powers. The beast that kills the two witnesses rises from this Abyss, showing that their opposition is not merely political but has its source in the deep, chaotic realm under God's judgment.

proskyneo.gloss: To worship, literally to bow down or prostrate oneself before someone in reverence. At the sound of the seventh trumpet the twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship God, the fitting response of heaven's representatives to the announcement that His kingdom has fully come.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, kingship, royal rule. The trumpet's declaration that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ marks the theological center of the whole book, the point toward which every judgment and every act of witness has been moving.

basic.context: John is handed a measuring rod and told to measure God's true temple and its worshipers, but to leave out the courtyard, which is given over to the nations to trample for a set time. Then God empowers two witnesses to prophesy in sackcloth for the same length of time, with power to shut the sky and strike the earth with plagues, like Elijah and Moses. A beast from the Abyss eventually kills them, and their enemies celebrate publicly and refuse them burial, until after three and a half days God raises them and takes them up to heaven in full view of their enemies, followed by an earthquake. Then the seventh trumpet sounds, and heaven declares that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and His Christ, who will reign forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter names hard, real experiences, God's people trampled, faithful witnesses killed and mocked, yet it ends in triumph, not defeat. Readers disagree over whether the two witnesses are two individual end-time prophets or a symbol of the whole church's costly testimony, and over how literally to take the numbers 42 months and 1,260 days, but nearly everyone agrees on the shape of the story: apparent defeat, public vindication, and the declared arrival of God's kingdom.
basic.originalLanguages: The naos, the inner sanctuary, and its worshipers are measured and kept safe, while the outer court is left to the nations. God's two martys, witnesses, prophesy at great cost, echoing the very word that gives us martyr. Their killer rises from the abyssos, the Abyss, showing a source deeper than mere human hostility. When the kingdom is announced, the elders proskyneo, worship, falling on their faces, because the basileia, the kingdom, of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and His Christ.
basic.typology: The two witnesses deliberately echo Moses, who turned water to blood, and Elijah, who shut up the sky, and they also recall Zechariah's two olive trees and two lampstands feeding oil to sustain the light of witness. Their death in the street of the very city where their Lord was crucified, and their resurrection after three and a half days, retells the pattern of Christ's own death and rising, now lived out in His witnessing church.
basic.christ: The witnesses are killed in the city 'where their Lord was also crucified,' tying their suffering directly to His. Their resurrection after three and a half days and their being taken up to heaven in a cloud mirror Christ's own death, resurrection, and ascension, showing that those who bear costly testimony to Him share both His sufferings and His vindication. The chapter's climax, that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ, names Jesus as the one to whom universal, everlasting reign now belongs.
basic.god: God measures and marks off His true worshipers even while permitting a season of trampling and apparent defeat. He empowers His witnesses, allows their death, and then, at exactly the right moment, breathes life back into them before a watching world. He is the Lord God Almighty who was and is and is to come, whose great power and reign, though sometimes seemingly delayed, arrives decisively and is publicly announced to all creation.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 4 pictures two olive trees supplying oil to the lampstand, an image reused for the two witnesses. 1 Kings 17 and Exodus 7 supply the miracles of shutting the sky and turning water to blood, tied to Elijah and Moses. And Daniel 7:14 anticipates the everlasting, universal dominion that the seventh trumpet here announces has arrived.
basic.oneStory: The people of God are measured and known even while the world tramples what looks unprotected, and His witnesses testify at the cost of their lives, only to be publicly vindicated exactly as their Lord was. This chapter is a small, vivid preview of the whole Bible's ending: suffering and apparent defeat give way, at God's appointed hour, to the open, joyful announcement that the kingdom of this world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, forever.

context.literary: This chapter closes out both the narrative interlude begun in chapter 10 and the entire trumpet series that started in chapter 8. Its structure measuring, prophesying, martyrdom, vindication, and trumpet mirrors the seal-cycle's own pattern of an interlude before a climactic seventh member, and its final verses about the ark and the temple in heaven open directly into the next major vision, the woman and the dragon of chapter 12.
context.historical: Written to churches under real pressure to compromise or fall silent, this scene assures them that faithful testimony, even to the point of death and public disgrace, is never wasted; the imagery of trampling nations, sackcloth, and a hostile beast from the Abyss reflects the real experience of a minority, prophetic community surrounded by an unbelieving, often hostile world.
context.canonical: The two witnesses gather up Israel's great prophetic pairs, Moses and Elijah, and Zechariah's olive trees and lampstands, into a single symbol of empowered, costly testimony. The seventh trumpet's declaration answers Daniel 7's vision of an everlasting kingdom given to the Son of Man and his saints, marking this chapter as a hinge between the trumpet judgments and the deeper unveiling of the dragon's war on the church that follows.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure suffering believers that God measures, knows, and ultimately protects His true worshipers, that faithful testimony will be met with real opposition and even death, but that such testimony is never defeated, and to announce, ahead of the final judgment, that God's kingdom has decisively come in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes a pattern of costly witness, apparent defeat, and public vindication; it is not a literal blueprint for two specific end-time individuals to be identified in advance. Its abiding truth calls every generation of God's people to faithful testimony, whatever the cost, trusting the same vindication.

debates.question: Who are the two witnesses?

views.label: Two individual end-time prophets
views.case: Their miracles closely match Elijah, who shut the sky, and Moses, who turned water to blood, and Jewish tradition expected such figures to return before the end; on this view they are two specific, individual persons raised up in the last days to confront a hostile world with undeniable, personal testimony.

views.label: A symbol of the church's corporate testimony
views.case: The number two recalls the biblical requirement that testimony be established by two witnesses, and the two olive trees and lampstands echo Zechariah's whole covenant community empowered by the Spirit to shine and testify; on this view the witnesses picture the church throughout the age, prophesying, suffering, and being vindicated together.

debates.bearing: Whether two individuals or a corporate symbol, the passage calls God's people to costly, Spirit-empowered testimony that ends not in defeat but in resurrection and open vindication before a watching world.

debates.question: How should the 42 months, 1,260 days, and the timing of this whole scene be understood, across the major interpretive schools?

views.label: Preterist
views.case: The trampling of the holy city and the death of the witnesses are tied to the actual siege and fall of Jerusalem around AD 70, a real, bounded period of Roman conquest and Jewish suffering that the first readers were living through or anticipating.

views.label: Historicist
views.case: The 1,260 days represent a long symbolic era within church history, often identified with centuries of suppressed or persecuted true testimony before a period of revival and vindication for God's faithful witnesses.

views.label: Futurist
views.case: The scene depicts a still-future tribulation period of exactly three and a half years, during which two specific prophets will testify, be killed, and be publicly and visibly raised before the final judgment.

views.label: Idealist
views.case: The numbers symbolize a limited, God-appointed season of suffering and testimony recurring throughout the church age, a pattern true in every generation rather than a chronology tied to one historical moment.

debates.bearing: All four traditions agree that whatever the length or timing, the period of trampling and testimony is bounded by God's sovereign appointment, and it ends, without exception, in vindication and the open arrival of God's kingdom.

study.originalLanguages: John measures the naos, the inner sanctuary, and its true worshipers, while the outer courtyard is deliberately left unmeasured and surrendered to the nations. God's two martys, witnesses, prophesy in sackcloth at the cost of their lives, a word that gives the language its very term for martyr. Their enemy comes up from the abyssos, the Abyss, the place of imprisoned hostile powers, showing the true depth of the opposition they face. And when the seventh trumpet sounds, the elders proskyneo, worship, prostrate before God, because the basileia, the kingdom, of the world has at last become the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ.

typology.type: Two witnesses with the powers of Moses and Elijah
typology.fulfillment: Their power to turn water to blood and shut up the sky recalls Exodus 7 and 1 Kings 17, presenting the church's testimony as standing in the line of Israel's greatest prophets.

typology.type: Death in the city where their Lord was crucified, then rising after three days
typology.fulfillment: Their pattern of death, public shame, and resurrection after three and a half days directly retells the death and resurrection of Jesus, now reenacted in His suffering, witnessing people.

typology.type: The everlasting kingdom given to the Son of Man
typology.fulfillment: Daniel 7:14's vision of an everlasting dominion given to one like a son of man and to the saints is the very kingdom the seventh trumpet announces has now become the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ.

study.christ: The two witnesses are killed 'where their Lord was also crucified,' explicitly binding their suffering to His, and their resurrection after three and a half days followed by ascension in a cloud retraces His own death, resurrection, and ascension. The chapter's climax names Him directly: the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, and He will reign forever and ever. Every faithful witness who suffers for the truth shares in the pattern and, ultimately, the vindication of Christ Himself.
study.god: God measures and protects His true worshipers even while permitting a season in which the world tramples what appears unguarded. He empowers costly testimony, allows real death and public disgrace, and then, at His appointed hour, breathes life into His witnesses before their enemies' own eyes. He is hailed as the Lord God Almighty, the One who is and who was, whose great power and reign have now visibly begun, bringing judgment on the destroyers of the earth and reward to His prophets and saints.

crossReferences.note: The two olive trees and two lampstands, supplying oil to sustain the light of testimony, the direct source of the imagery for the two witnesses.
Elijah's power to shut the sky and Moses' power to turn water to blood, both echoed in the witnesses' own powers.
The everlasting kingdom given to one like a son of man, the vision fulfilled in the seventh trumpet's declaration that the kingdom has become the Lord's and His Christ's.
The time to judge the dead, announced here as having come, is carried out in full at the final judgment before the great white throne.

study.oneStory: God knows and measures His true worshipers even in seasons when the world seems to trample everything sacred, and He empowers costly, prophetic testimony that the world will hate and even kill, exactly as it killed His own Son in that same city. But death is never the last word: the witnesses rise, are taken up before watching enemies, and the whole chapter ends with heaven's long-awaited announcement, that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, who will reign forever and ever, the goal toward which the entire biblical story has always been moving.

quiz.q: What is John told to do with the courtyard outside the temple?

options.text: Exclude it from the measuring, because it has been given over to the nations to trample for 42 months.
options.note: Revelation 11:2 gives this exact instruction.

options.text: Measure it more carefully than the temple itself.
options.note: The opposite is commanded; the courtyard is specifically excluded from measuring.

options.text: Destroy it immediately.
options.note: John is told to measure or not measure, not to destroy anything himself.

options.text: Give it to the twenty-four elders.
options.note: It is given over to the trampling nations, not to the elders.

quiz.q: What imagery from the Old Testament describes the two witnesses?

options.text: The two olive trees and two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.
options.note: Revelation 11:4 draws this directly from Zechariah's vision.

options.text: The two tablets of the Ten Commandments.
options.note: The text specifically names olive trees and lampstands, not tablets.

options.text: The two cherubim over the ark of the covenant.
options.note: Cherubim are not the image used here; olive trees and lampstands are.

options.text: The two pillars of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The passage names olive trees and lampstands from Zechariah's vision, not the temple pillars.

quiz.q: What happens to the two witnesses after they finish their testimony?

options.text: The beast from the Abyss wages war on them, overpowers them, and kills them.
options.note: Revelation 11:7 states this directly.

options.text: They are welcomed as heroes by the nations.
options.note: The opposite occurs; their enemies gloat over their deaths rather than honoring them.

options.text: They retire peacefully and are never heard from again.
options.note: They are killed publicly, and then dramatically raised and taken to heaven, not left in quiet retirement.

options.text: They are given a proper burial by the nations.
options.note: The text says the opposite, their enemies refuse to let their bodies be laid in a tomb.

quiz.q: What happens three and a half days after the witnesses are killed?

options.text: The breath of life from God enters them, they stand up, and they are taken up to heaven in a cloud before their enemies.
options.note: Revelation 11:11-12 describes this resurrection and ascension.

options.text: Their bodies simply disappear without explanation.
options.note: The text specifically describes God reviving them and their visible ascension, not a mere disappearance.

options.text: The nations finally bury them with honor.
options.note: Instead, God raises them publicly rather than the nations changing their treatment of the bodies.

options.text: They are replaced by two new witnesses.
options.note: The same two witnesses are raised and ascend; they are not replaced.

quiz.q: What is announced when the seventh trumpet sounds?

options.text: That the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ, who will reign forever and ever.
options.note: Revelation 11:15 records this declaration exactly.

options.text: That the trumpet judgments are cancelled.
options.note: Far from cancelling judgment, the trumpet announces the arrival of God's reign and the time to judge and to reward.

options.text: That the beast from the Abyss has won.
options.note: The declaration is the opposite, the victory and reign belong to the Lord and His Christ.

options.text: That the temple in heaven will remain closed.
options.note: Revelation 11:19 says the temple of God in heaven was opened, not kept closed.

questions: God measured and marked off His true worshipers even while the outer courtyard was given over to trampling. Where do you need to trust that God knows and protects what looks unguarded in your own life?

The two witnesses testified faithfully even though it cost them their lives, and their enemies celebrated their deaths. What would faithful testimony cost you this week, and are you willing to pay it?
The witnesses were publicly humiliated for three and a half days before God raised and vindicated them. How does their story help you wait through seasons when faithfulness seems to end in defeat?
The seventh trumpet declares that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and His Christ. How would living as though that is already true change your priorities this week?
The elders responded to God's reign with worship and thanksgiving. What is one specific way you can respond to God's sovereign rule with worship today rather than anxiety?

translationNotes.note: 42 months, 11:3's 1,260 days, and 12:14's 'a time, and times, and half a time' are parallel ways of naming the same symbolic span, roughly three and a half years, half of the number seven, picturing a limited, broken period of trial rather than necessarily a precise, literal calendar length.

'Figuratively' translates a Greek term meaning spiritually or symbolically, signaling that Sodom and Egypt are not literal place names for the city but theological labels for its moral corruption and opposition to God.
'Three and a half days' is a short, symbolic mirror of the longer three and a half years already mentioned, underlining that even a brief period of apparent triumph for evil is firmly bounded by God.
'Loud voices' rather than a single voice suggests a great heavenly chorus, matching the scale of the announcement that the kingdom of the world has become the Lord's.

meta.notes: BSB verse text for Revelation 11:1-19 was supplied pre-verified this session by the requester; no refetching was performed. Any em or en dash conversions to commas and the flattening of poetic line breaks into flowing prose were already done in the supplied text before this file was written. No em or en dashes were introduced in this file's own prose.
